reviews.review_text: Bardzo polecam grę, fajnie sie bawiłem podbijając cały świat III rzeszą

It's to a paradox game as the hungry hungry caterpillar is to paradise lost.

1 unit can conquer a province so the entirety of the "game" here is making sure you have no gaps, else 30 dudes conquer all of Eurasia.

That's it. Biggest stack wins. No unit bonus, stats, trees, terrain bonuses, sieges, zones of control, trade etc. Just make sure you have no gaps or the AI will *teleports behind you* and take down your nation with 200 troops (literally, the AI doesn't keep track of actual movement behind fog of war). 

Pretty much all you need to know. Its a fidget spinner, a tism tickler, not a strategy game. If you like patiently sorting things by shape an color you will enjoy. 
If you want to creatively problem solve, you will not.

Plays as a weak nation
Gets attacked by a much stronger nation
Pays stronger nation's closest allies 1 gold each to declare war on stronger nation
Wins
10/10 would recommend
Get used to clicking alot.. its pretty boring and not much to do.

I hate this game. It has so many annoying little bugs or flaws that really mess things up for me. Sometimes on turn 1000-2000 all relations break and suddenly the whole world is neutral of me. Spawning vassals can reset a province, which means the pop and economy suddenly drop for no good reason. Bots waste their populations in constant war, and will always attack the exact same province when making a naval landing, reducing a big city to 9000 people with one small shopping mall. It's also fairly barebones, lacking many features bigger games have.

That said, it perfectly nails the one more turn dynamic, and you may have noticed I have spent a lot of hours on this. I am addicted to it, its such a simple game with straightforward systems that are easy to learn and abuse. Its fun picking a game start and playing through it each time. Sometimes you dont want a complex, intelligent game to play through a story with, like Civ or CKIII. Sometimes you just want a simple, enjoyable little game, and this game perfectly scratches that itch.

This game is a lot better than it seems. It is simplistic yet addictive. There is much fun to be had with different scenarios offering different styles of gameplay. The game has many great mechanics like dealing with inflation, economic spending, integration etc. I have had a lot of fun playing this game, and would honestly recommend it. 

7/10
i havent really found it fun myself tbh, i would recommend getting something like dummynation or something else, maybe HOI4, CK3, EU4, VIC2, ect or even Age of History III or even Ages of Conflict

I played this game for a few hours after I bought it and I didn't enjoy my time. After coming back to it a few months later I rediscovered a hidden gem in my library and I can confidently say that Age of History II has earned it's place as the best game I've ever personally played.

The game play in Age of History II is unlike anything that I've ever played. You can feel like the most powerful creature in the game while still knowing that you are only one mistake away from losing. The game is difficult in an unfair way that can initially be very frustrating as it feels that you have no control over your environment. One of the best parts of this game is it's simulated environments. Due to the fact that everything is simulated independently from you, it really makes you feel like you're just an observer in a real world. The country AI in this game is really detailed and helps the feeling of an actual Earth with both predator and prey.

You play this game as a creature known as a country. The best feeling in this game is the feel of getting stronger, not from in-game upgrades or weapons, but from your skill increasing and learning new mechanics and techniques with the tools available to you. The movement in this game is procedurally generated, which leads to the movement sometimes feeling unnatural or clunky, but then as you learn new movement techniques the movement becomes far more fluid and natural, which leads to some of the best feeling movements in a game that I've ever played.

There's so much more to this game that I haven't covered here, but I'll leave that up to you to decide if you would like to try the game and become more familiar with it. This game is truly one-of-a-kind and itself is an experience. It certainly isn't for everyone but it is so unique and enjoyable that if you can make it past the first few hours of game play and get the game to "click" with you, then you may just find one of the best gaming experiences that you've ever had.

7/10. Better than AoH3, but not that great. Very little bugs, pretty simple and easy to understand. I like it for how casual it is, but at the same time, at some points it may feel too casual (just have higher number of soldiers than the enemy and you will win, the most amount of strategy you can use is standing on a province with a castle and watching the enemy endlessly end themselves against the castle).
Good and addictive game, takes a couple of hours to learn but its definitely worth it
